Don’t forget to save each time with Ctrl+S on PC or Cmd+S on Mac!

Reopen the Git tools and you’ll see a summary of your changes. Click a file to view the details:

You have a few options here, but let’s say you want to commit your changes. First, stage them by clicking the + icon next to each file. Then enter your commit message (for example, “Changed language”), and press Ctrl+Enter on PC or Cmd+Enter on Mac to commit.

All the version-control commands are available in this VS Code panel (and via the command palette), so feel free to try them with your own repositories.

You can even connect your GitHub account.

Use Extensions

To further customize VS Code, you can install plugins, also known as extensions. Extensions add features and tools to your editor.

You can browse all extensions at https://marketplace.visualstudio.com. Here are two I recommend:

  • Live Server – one of the most popular VS Code extensions. It starts a development server and refreshes your page in the browser whenever you save changes; and

  • Git Lens – another popular extension that makes it easier to access many Git and GitHub features.

To install an extension, open Extensions on the left side of the window and type the name of the extension you want:

Click an extension to read its description, then click the Install button to add it.

Discover More VS Code Extensions

Here are a few more VS Code extensions you may find useful:

  • Live Share – collaborate with other developers in real time by working on the same files at the same time for easy pair programming.

  • French Language Pack for Visual Studio Code – Microsoft’s official extension for translating the VS Code interface into French.

  • Stories – like Instagram stories, but with code!

  • Search Hero – since every developer searches the web for answers, Search Hero integrates search and multi-engine translation directly into VS Code.

You can, of course, find all available extensions in the Visual Studio Marketplace.

Summary

In this chapter, you explored additional VS Code features:

  • you explored different ways to use the integrated terminal;

  • you worked with Git repositories; and

  • you installed your first extensions.

Of course, you’ll continue learning this tool throughout your studies and career, and you’ll discover more advanced features over time—but this is already a great start.

In the next chapter, you’ll learn how to test your code using Chrome and Firefox devtools!

Test Your Code with Chrome and Firefox Devtools

In this chapter, you’ll use the developer tools—or devtools—in Chrome and Firefox to analyze a page on the OpenClassrooms website.

Explore the Inspector: HTML

Using an OpenClassrooms web page, you’ll see what the HTML inspector looks like in your browser.

To get started, open https://openclassrooms.com/fr/paths in Chrome:

To inspect the structure of this page, you’ll open the element inspector. On Windows, press Ctrl+Maj+C. On macOS, press Option+Cmd+C. The inspector will open at the bottom of your screen.

The shortcuts are the same in Chrome and Firefox, and all the actions in this section are identical, with slight differences in icon design (but everything is in the same place in both browsers).

For readability, I prefer docking the devtools on the right side of my screen. To change the dock position, use the option in the customization menu:

The code you see in the inspector is HTML—the language that defines the structure of a web page.

A web page is made up of elements: text, images, buttons, and more. An element can be a parent to multiple child elements. For example, the header on this page contains child elements like the OpenClassrooms logo, links, a Search button, and sign-in buttons.

When you hover over different lines of code, the corresponding elements will be highlighted on the page. For example, hover over this line:

<div id="oc-header" data-widget="header">...</div>

The page header will be highlighted:

This tells you that the line of code you hovered over corresponds to the page header. Click the arrow at the beginning of that line to expand it:

You’ll see that the div element contains a single child: a header element. This page structure includes several elements with a single child. Expand them until you find the multiple children that correspond to the different header items:

At this level, hover over the different child elements. You’ll see each one highlighted on the page, which helps you match each line of code to its visual element:

You can also locate an element’s code by right-clicking it on the page and selecting Inspect. Try it on the text “Découvrez nos parcours diplômants”:

You can also enable “inspect element” mode from within the inspector so you can hover and click elements on the page to inspect them:

You now know how to inspect the structural elements of a web page. Keep the selection you just made—we’ll use it to explore another part of the inspector: styles.

Explore the Inspector: Styles

Now take a look at what the styles inspector looks like in your browser.

The browser inspector lets you view and edit the code that controls a page’s styles—in other words, the appearance of its elements. If you changed your selection in the previous section, reselect the “Découvrez nos parcours diplômants” element. In the lower part of the inspector, you’ll see the Styles section:

On Chrome

On Firefox

Feel free to increase the height of this section. In Firefox, I also recommend disabling the three-pane view using this button in the Styles inspector header:

In the styles inspector (“Styles” in Chrome, “Rules” in Firefox), you can filter styles to find what you need—and even change values.

For example, let’s say you want to change the text’s white color. In the filter field, type “color,” then find the color rule with a small white square and the value rgb(255, 255, 255):

On Chrome

On Firefox

Click the value next to color to edit it. Type red, and you’ll see the element’s appearance update:

Editing styles on the fly is a powerful way to test changes without touching your project’s source code.

Explore the Console

Another key devtools feature is the console. It gives you crucial information about what’s happening in your project—especially when something goes wrong. You’ll find it in the devtools header.

On Chrome

On Firefox

As a front-end developer, you can also log messages to the console. For example, OpenClassrooms developers display a warning message to alert potential victims of a self-XSS attack, where an attacker tricks someone into pasting malicious code into the console.

At the time this course was created, you can also see warnings related to the source code—and even an error!

The console will probably be one of the devtools features you use most often.

Enable Mobile Mode

About 50% of web traffic today happens on mobile, so as a front-end developer, it’s essential to check how your projects look on different devices. Here’s how to work with mobile layouts:

You’ll often hear the term responsive design. It’s an approach where a page or app’s layout adapts to the device it’s viewed on—often based on the browser window width in pixels.

Devtools let you simulate many mobile devices fairly accurately. You can enable mobile mode from the devtools header:

On Chrome

On Firefox

You’ll see the active browsing area resize. And you’ll notice the OpenClassrooms developers did a great job—this design works well on mobile:

In mobile mode, the browser header lets you choose which device to simulate, switch orientation, and even throttle bandwidth to simulate a slow connection.

All of these tools will be essential when you build front-end projects.

Summary

In this chapter:

  • you analyzed a web page’s structure using the inspector;

  • you found and edited an element’s styles;

  • you explored the console; and

  • you used mobile mode to simulate a mobile device.

Congratulations—you’ve reached the end of the course. You’ve set up your development environment, installed Visual Studio Code, Chrome, and Firefox, and built a solid foundation for using their core features.
Now you’re ready to review what you’ve learned with the quiz I prepared for you!

Wishing you all the best as you continue your front-end development journey!
